titlestmt.titleproper: A guide to the Dwight and Julia Marsh family papers AD.MC.152
titlestmt.author: Brad Bauer

publicationstmt.publisher: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

address.addressline: New York University Abu Dhabi, C-2

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-05-04 17:32:42 +0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

did.unittitle: Dwight and Julia Marsh family papers
did.abstract: Correspondence, journal, and related items, documenting the experiences of American Protestant missionaries Dwight W. Marsh and Julia (née Peck) Marsh, in Mosul, Assyria during the 1850s. Correspondence to family members in the United States recounts their life in Mosul during this period, and journal accounts document their trips between Boston and Izmir (Smyrna) in 1852 and 1853.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions governing access
accessrestrict.p: The Dwight and Julia Marsh family papers are open for use in the Archives and Special Collections Reading Room at New York University Abu Dhabi.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The correspondence is largely arranged in chronological order, by correspondent, in three series, with a fourth series containing a photograph, journal entries, and later copied material.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: Dwight Whitney Marsh (1823-1896) was born in Dalton, Massachusetts on 5 November 1823, the son of Henry and Sarah (née Whitney) Marsh. His siblings included his brothers Henry (1822-1852), Calvin Waldo (b. 1825), and Robert (1828-1828); and his sisters included Elizabeth (b. 1829), Clarissa (b. 1834), and Sarah (1836-1841). Not much is known about his early life, other than that he graduated from Williams College in 1842, and moved to St. Louis, Missouri, the following year, where his brother Henry was a merchant, and he worked as a teacher there until 1847. He returned to studies for the ordained ministry, obtaining degrees from Andover Theological Seminary (1845) and Union Theological Seminary (1849), and after obtaining the latter degree, he set off for Mosul, Assyria (modern-day Iraq), in the Ottoman Empire, to serve as a Christian missionary under the auspices of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions.

Much less is known about Marsh's wife, the former Julia White Peck (1829-1859), who was born in Hartford, Connecticut on 10 June 1829, the daughter of Mary Jane Averill and Elisa Peck, both of Hartford. At some point, as a young woman, she lived with her family in New York City, and in one of her letters she recalls having met Marsh in July 1849 while he was also living in New York, and studying at Union Theological Seminary. They likely became engaged to be married either before or shortly after he left for Mosul, and other details of her life and their relationship can be deduced from the present correspondence.

In late spring 1852, Marsh left Mosul, and traveled back to the United States by way of Izmir, in a journey described in his correspondence. Prior to that, in addition to his missionary work based largely in and around Mosul, he had also played a role in the excavation of the ruins at nearby Nineveh, assisting the English Assyriologist, Austen Henry Layard. While many of Layard's finds were sent to England and were added to the holdings of the British Museum, two stone reliefs from the 9th century BCE, or "slabs" as they are referred to in Marsh's correspondence, were sent back to his alma mater, Williams College, where they are now part of that institution's museum of art.

Marsh arrived back in the United States in the late summer of 1852, and spent time visiting family and friends in upstate New York and Ohio prior to his and Julia's wedding on 10 October 1852. Following their wedding, they made a journey of two months that took them from New England to the Great Lakes region, all the way to Racine, Wisconsin and then southward to St. Louis, where Dwight Marsh's family lived at that time. After a journey up the Ohio River and back to Boston, from which they set sail in early January 1853, they arrived in Izmir a month later, and following stays in Latakia and Beirut, they arrived in Mosul in the late spring of 1853.

The activities of the Marshes were typical of Protestant missionaries in that region during this period. While Dwight Marsh led worship services at a church in Mosul and made trips to towns and villages in the surrounding region, even as far as Urmia (present-day Iran), Julia Marsh worked as a homemaker and occasionally taught Sunday school classes at church, and classes for local women. Dwight Marsh's activities were focused largely on the local population of Nestorian Christians as well as Muslims, and reflects the efforts of 19th century Protestant missionaries in this region to gain influence among the local Nestorians. During their years in Mosul, the Marsh's had two children, who both died in Mosul: Henry (1854-1854) and Waldo (1856-1859). The topic of Julia's struggles with ill health surface repeatedly in the correspondence from Mosul, and on 12 August 1859, she died just a couple of months short of her thirtieth birthday. One year later, Dwight left the mission field and returned to the United States, and the Protestant mission in Mosul appears to have ended at that time as well.

For the remaining decades of his life, Dwight Marsh served as a minister at various churches throughout the United States, including in Godfrey (Monticello Station), IL (1867-1868), Whitneys Point, NY (1869-1871), Oswego, NY (1871-1876), Amherst (1876-1878), and Haydenville, MA (1878-1882). He married in 1862, and with his second wife, Elizabeth Le Baron Clarke, he had three further children. During this time, the Marshes also served as principals of the Rochester (NY) Seminary for Young Ladies, from 1862-1869. Marsh periodically lectured on behalf of the Mission Board, and published a biography of fellow missionary Samuel Audley Rhea, titled "The Tennesseean in Persia and Koordistan: being the scenes and incidents in the life of Samuel Audley Rhea" (Philadelphia : Presbyterian Publication Committee, 1869).
Dwight W. Marsh died in Amherst, Massachusetts, where he had lived during the last decade of his life, on 18 June 1896.
Sources: "Marsh genealogy: Giving several thousand descendants of John Marsh of Hartford, Ct 1636-1895;" accessed in Ancestry.com, February 2026.
"Averill Family History," p. 734-735; accessed on Ancestry.com, February 2026

"Stones of Assyria : Ancient spirits from the Palace of Ashurnasirpal II," exhibition catalogue, Williams College Museum of Art, 2001. https://artmuseum.williams.edu/stonesofassyria , accessed March 2026.

prefercite.head: Preferred citation
prefercite.p: [item], [folder number], Dwight and Julia Marsh family papers, MC-152, New York University Abu Dhabi Library, Abu Dhabi, UAE.

processinfo.head: Processing information
processinfo.p: Processed by Brad Bauer, December 2025 - January 2026.

relatedmaterial.head: Related materials
relatedmaterial.p: A related collection of Marsh family correspondence, including letters from Dwight W. Marsh, is held in the Archives and Special Collections of Brock University Library, St. Catherine's, Ontario (Canada), as RG 854, the "Dwight Whitney Marsh family papers." For further information, see: https://brocku.ca/library/archives/

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Dwight and Julia Marsh Family Papers consist of correspondence, journal entries, and related material, documenting the experiences of American Protestant missionaries Dwight W. Marsh and Julia (née Peck) Marsh, in Ottoman-ruled Assyria (present day Iraq) during the 1850s. The bulk of the collection is comprised of correspondence to family members in the United States describing their lives in Mosul during this period, and journal accounts documenting their trips between Boston and Izmir (Smyrna) in 1852 and 1853.

The correspondence, while covering the years 1850-1860, is focused chiefly on the earlier years of that decade. The letters include those sent from Dwight to Julia and to his family, during his time in Mosul and on his return journey to the United States in 1852; Julia's separate correspondence to Dwight's family in St. Louis from 1850-1852; and their joint correspondence following their wedding in October 1852, as they traveled around the United States to visit family and before setting sail for Turkey in early 1853. The early correspondence is characterized by Dwight's spiritual reflections and concerns about his family, in particular about the spiritual welfare of his brother, Waldo. Julia's correspondence to Dwight's family often reflects her role as a mediator of information about Dwight, which she first learned of from the missionary board, and from whom she received his journals, which she sent along to his family with her own correspondence. Sometimes she shared information that only she was privy to, such as the details of a Kurdish attack on Dwight and some of his colleagues, which they suffered during a trip to Urmia, and which was written about in American newspapers, and from which Julia sought to reassure Dwight's family that he escaped unharmed (letter of July 21, 1851). In many of her letters she talked of her own desire to assist Dwight in his ministry in Mosul after their impending wedding, looking forward to times when she can "stand with him in our home in Asia and comfort his heart and strengthen his hands as he strives to communicate to [the] darkened minds the light of life" (July 4, 1851).

The largest group of letters dates from their time together in the Middle East from 1853-1855, beginning with their arrival in Izmir (Smyrna), their stopover and quarantine in Beirut, and their subsequent arrival in Mosul. This correspondence depicts Julia's efforts to adjust to life in Mosul, her impressions of her new surroundings, and of their building of a new house there. Also included are descriptions of Dwight's ministry in and around Mosul, including church services, encounters with communities of Nestorian Christians, and trips to outlying villages. Occasional references are made to the "ruins of Nineveh" and the "slabs" that were excavated there, The birth of their first child, Henry, is described in correspondence from 1854, as is his subsequent death, and the grief that Julia experiences and as well as attempts to find solace in her faith, noting that his "grave is beneath the hot sands of this wicked Moslem city, but his little spirit free, glorious, pure and beautiful dwells with Jesus." During this same period, Dwight's letters to family also reflect on larger events in the world, such as the Crimean War, which he fears will spread to their corner of the Ottoman Empire, as well as his concerns about events in the United States, such as the evils of slavery and the fights between anti- and pro-slavery forces in Kansas. Save for a signle letter in from 1857, the correspondence in this collection leaves off in 1855, only to resume with a few letters following Julia's death in 1859 and Dwight's journey back to the United States.

userestrict.head: Conditions governing use
userestrict.p: The content in this collection can be deemed to be out of copyright term, and in the public domain, yet any quotations or excerpts from these materials that are used in publicatin should cite this collection as their source.
